questions.stem: Which task is most appropriate for the nurse to delegate to an assistive personnel (AP)?

options.text: Measuring and recording a stable patient's vital signs
Teaching a patient about a new medication
Assessing chest pain
Evaluating a new wound drainage change

questions.rationale: Vital signs on a stable patient are a routine, predictable task that can be delegated when the nurse retains responsibility for follow-up. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Teaching requires nursing judgment. - C Assessment of chest pain must be done by the nurse. - D Wound changes need nursing assessment.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "Delegate routine data collection, not assessment or teaching" suggests infection progression, so "Teaching a patient about a new medication" would not correct the active problem. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.c: "Assessing chest pain" misses the bedside risk. The cue "- C Assessment of chest pain must be done by the nurse" points to infection progression. Infection control or sepsis escalation limits spread and organ hypoperfusion before lower-acuity care.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave infection progression untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Measuring and recording a stable patient's vital signs" fits the safer response to the cue "**Why the others are wrong:** - B Teaching requires nursing judgment".

questions.takeaway: Delegate routine data collection, not assessment or teaching.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A patient suddenly says, "I can't catch my breath," and is leaning forward. What should the nurse do first?

options.text: Assess airway, breathing, and oxygenation immediately
Tell the patient to lie flat and relax
Wait to see if the feeling passes
Focus on obtaining a full history first

questions.rationale: Sudden dyspnea can signal acute respiratory distress. The nurse should assess breathing and oxygenation before anything less urgent. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Lying flat can worsen breathing for some patients. - C Delay can miss a serious problem. - D A focused assessment comes before a long history.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Tell the patient to lie flat and relax" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The cue "The nurse should assess breathing and oxygenation before anything less urgent" suggests oxygenation or ventilation, so "Wait to see if the feeling passes" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Focus on obtaining a full history first" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.

questions.takeaway: Sudden breathing changes require immediate reassessment.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A patient on oxygen has a falling oxygen saturation trend and is now more short of breath. What is the best nursing concern?

options.text: The respiratory status may be worsening
The patient is improving because oxygen is still on
A single normal reading later will cancel the trend
Breathing changes only matter if the patient is anxious

questions.rationale: A worsening trend matters even if oxygen is already being used. The nurse should reassess the patient and the support. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Oxygen use does not guarantee stability. - C A trend should not be ignored. - D Anxiety is not the only explanation for shortness of breath.

distractorRationales.b: "The patient is improving because oxygen is still on" misses the bedside risk. The cue "A worsening trend matters even if oxygen is already being used" points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave oxygenation or ventilation untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "The respiratory status may be worsening" fits the safer response to the cue "A worsening trend matters even if oxygen is already being used".
distractorRationales.d: "Breathing changes only matter if the patient is anxious" misses the bedside risk. The cue "A worsening trend matters even if oxygen is already being used" points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: Trends often matter more than one isolated number.

visualRationale.caption: Trends often matter more than one isolated number.
visualRationale.conclusion: Trends often matter more than one isolated number.

questions.stem: After surgery, a patient has shallow breathing, a low oxygen saturation, and is reluctant to cough because of pain. What is the best interpretation?

options.text: The patient may be developing postoperative respiratory compromise
The patient is stable because pain is the main issue
The oxygen saturation is not meaningful after surgery
Coughing should be avoided completely

questions.rationale: Pain can limit deep breathing and coughing, which increases the risk of atelectasis and respiratory decline. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Pain and breathing are linked here. - C Oxygen saturation remains important. - D Incentive breathing and cough are often part of recovery.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "Pain can limit deep breathing and coughing, which increases the risk of atelectasis and respiratory decline" suggests oxygenation or ventilation, so "The patient is stable because pain is the main issue" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.c: "The oxygen saturation is not meaningful after surgery" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Pain can limit deep breathing and coughing, which increases the risk of atelectasis and respiratory decline" points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: "Coughing should be avoided completely" misses the bedside risk. The cue "**Why the others are wrong:** - B Pain and breathing are linked here" points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: Pain-related shallow breathing can become a respiratory problem.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A patient receives a sedating medication and becomes less responsive than before. What should the nurse do first?

options.text: Reassess airway, breathing, and level of consciousness
Assume the sedation is expected and continue the routine
Leave the patient alone until the next scheduled round
Document the change only after the shift ends

questions.rationale: Any decrease in responsiveness after a sedating medication requires immediate reassessment for airway and breathing compromise. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Expected sedation still needs safety checks. - C Delay can miss deterioration. - D Documentation follows assessment, not the other way around.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Assume the sedation is expected and continue the routine" moves care toward routine observation before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.c: The cue "Any decrease in responsiveness after a sedating medication requires immediate reassessment for airway and breathing compromise" suggests oxygenation or ventilation, so "Leave the patient alone until the next scheduled round" would not correct the active problem. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "**Why the others are wrong:** - B Expected sedation still needs safety checks" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: After sedating meds, responsiveness and breathing must be watched closely.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A patient suddenly becomes difficult to arouse and is not following commands. What is the nurse's best action?

options.text: Perform a focused neurologic assessment and check for a reversible cause
Assume the patient is just tired
Wait until the next round to reassess
Offer food and fluids first

questions.rationale: An acute change in mental status may reflect stroke, hypoxia, glucose change, medication effect, or another urgent cause. **Why the others are wrong:** - B Sudden reduced responsiveness should not be dismissed. - C Delay can miss a serious decline. - D Food and fluids do not replace a neuro assessment.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "An acute change in mental status may reflect stroke, hypoxia, glucose change, medication effect, or another urgent cause" suggests unstable glucose physiology, so "Assume the patient is just tired" would not correct the active problem. Glucose instability can progress quickly, so medication timing and metabolic rescue come before routine follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: "Wait until the next round to reassess" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "A patient suddenly becomes difficult to arouse and is not following commands" raises concern for unstable glucose physiology, so glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.d: "Offer food and fluids first" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "A patient suddenly becomes difficult to arouse and is not following commands" raises concern for unstable glucose physiology, so glucose rescue or metabolic stabilization is needed before routine care.

questions.takeaway: New neurologic decline needs prompt assessment, not delay.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client reports severe eye pain, blurred vision, and seeing halos around lights. Which interpretation by the nurse is most accurate?

options.text: The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation.
This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue.
The client is likely having a migraine and can rest at home.
These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well.

questions.rationale: Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency that can threaten vision. The nurse should recognize the need for urgent ophthalmic evaluation rather than dismissing the symptoms as routine strain. Time-sensitive treatment matters for outcome.

distractorRationales.b: "This is an expected effect of routine eye fatigue" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma" raises concern for clinical safety risk, so bedside safety action is needed before routine observation.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave clinical safety risk untreated because it moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. "The client may have acute angle-closure glaucoma and needs urgent evaluation" fits the safer response to the cue "Severe eye pain with blurred vision and halos is concerning for acute angle-closure glaucoma, an ocular emergency".
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "These findings show that prescribed eye drops are working well" moves care toward routine care before bedside safety action.

questions.takeaway: Eye pain plus halos and blurred vision should trigger concern for acute angle-closure glaucoma.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg, and sodium 128 mEq/L. Which provider prescription should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids.
Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight.
Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn.
Teach the client about low-sodium foods.

questions.rationale: Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting, and weakness in a client with adrenal insufficiency suggest adrenal crisis. The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Delaying treatment for further testing can be life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: "Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight" moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. The cue "The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching.
distractorRationales.c: The cue "Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting" suggests circulation or perfusion, so "Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave circulation or perfusion untreated because it uses teaching before the physiologic risk is controlled. "Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids" fits the safer response to the cue "sodium 128 mEq/L".

questions.takeaway: Suspected adrenal crisis requires steroids and isotonic fluids right away.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives, and swelling of the lips. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Stop the infusion and prepare to administer epinephrine.
Obtain a full allergy history before intervening.
Slow the infusion to reduce the reaction.
Document the event and continue monitoring.

questions.rationale: Wheezing, hives, and lip swelling indicate anaphylaxis. The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise and shock can develop quickly. Delaying definitive treatment can be fatal.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses oxygenation or ventilation; "Obtain a full allergy history before intervening" moves care toward routine care before airway and breathing intervention.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of airway and breathing intervention. The stem cue "A client receiving IV antibiotics suddenly develops wheezing, widespread hives" makes oxygenation or ventilation the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: "Document the event and continue monitoring" misses the bedside risk. The cue "The causative infusion should be stopped immediately and epinephrine readied or administered per protocol because airway compromise" points to oxygenation or ventilation. Airway assessment, positioning, oxygen support, or rapid escalation protects ventilation before teaching or documentation.

questions.takeaway: When anaphylaxis begins, stop the trigger and act for airway and circulation immediately.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure of 208/110 mmHg. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention.
Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature.
Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes.
Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered by bladder distention. The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure.

distractorRationales.b: "Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: The safer response addresses neurologic deterioration; "Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes" moves care toward routine care before neurologic safety assessment.
distractorRationales.d: "Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before routine observation.

questions.takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia is a remove-the-trigger emergency, often starting with sitting the client up and checking the bladder.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A nurse begins a packed red blood cell transfusion for a client with postoperative anemia. Fifteen minutes later the client reports chills, low back pain, and chest tightness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Stop the transfusion and keep the IV line open with normal saline.
Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes.
Administer acetaminophen and continue the transfusion.
Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response.

questions.rationale: Chills, low back pain, and chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction. The nurse should stop the blood immediately to prevent further exposure and maintain IV access with normal saline using new tubing so emergency treatment can be given. Slowing the infusion or masking symptoms delays the response to a potentially life-threatening complication.

distractorRationales.b: "Slow the transfusion rate and reassess in 5 minutes" misses the bedside risk. The cue "Suspected transfusion reaction means stop the blood first and keep the line open with normal saline" points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "Chills, low back pain" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The cue "chest tightness shortly after starting blood are red-flag findings for an acute transfusion reaction" suggests circulation or perfusion, so "Document the symptoms as an expected febrile response" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Suspected transfusion reaction means stop the blood first and keep the line open with normal saline.

questions.stem: A client with profuse diarrhea tests positive for Clostridioides difficile. Which infection-control action should the nurse implement?

options.text: Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves.
Use alcohol-based sanitizer alone because it is faster.
Place the client on droplet precautions only.
Share the blood pressure cuff after wiping it with a dry towel.

questions.rationale: C. difficile spores are not reliably removed by alcohol-based hand rub alone, so soap-and-water hand hygiene is required after glove removal. Contact precautions and dedicated equipment are also important. Using only sanitizer or shared equipment increases transmission risk.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave infection progression untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves" fits the safer response to the cue "c diff spore precautions infection".
distractorRationales.c: "Place the client on droplet precautions only" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "c diff spore precautions infection" raises concern for infection progression, so infection or sepsis escalation is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave infection progression untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "Use soap and water for hand hygiene after removing gloves" fits the safer response to the cue "c diff spore precautions infection".

questions.takeaway: C. difficile care requires soap-and-water hand hygiene and contact precautions.
